config.title: Holocaust Memorial Berlin - FAQ
config.subtitle: Quick answers about visiting the Field of Stelae, Information Centre and nearby route respectfully.

items.q: Is the Holocaust Memorial Berlin free?
items.a: Yes. The Field of Stelae is free and accessible from the street, and the underground Information Centre is also free. The Information Centre has opening hours, security checks and last-admission rules, so check the official page on the day.

items.q: Is the Information Centre at the Holocaust Memorial Berlin open?
items.a: As checked on 4 July 2026, the Stiftung Denkmal states that the Information Centre is open again after renovation, with regular hours Tuesday to Sunday, 10:00-18:00. Last admission is normally 45 minutes before closing.

items.q: How long do I need at the Holocaust Memorial Berlin?
items.a: For the Field of Stelae only, plan around 20 to 30 minutes. For the field plus the Information Centre, plan about 75 to 90 minutes because the exhibition itself takes roughly one hour and may involve security waiting time.

items.q: What should I not do at the Holocaust Memorial Berlin?
items.a: Do not jump from stele to stele, climb, make noise, smoke, drink alcohol, bring pets into the field or use it as a bike/equipment storage space. Cross the field slowly and on foot.

items.q: Can I take photos at the Holocaust Memorial Berlin?
items.a: Photos in the field are possible, but use judgment. A quiet wide photo is different from posing, climbing or turning the memorial into content. Inside the Information Centre, follow the posted rules and avoid flash.

items.q: Should I visit the Information Centre with children?
items.a: The foundation recommends the exhibition for young people from 14. The field is public space, but the underground exhibition is heavy and should not be treated like light background sightseeing.

items.q: Can I combine the Holocaust Memorial Berlin with a BerlinWalk tour?
items.a: Yes, but choose the timing carefully. The BerlinWalk tour lasts about **2 hours** and covers central Berlin layers around Alexanderplatz, Museum Island and the historic centre. A quiet field visit can work before or after; the full Information Centre may be better when you have proper attention.
